data.title: Soaring With SOR? Let's Run The Numbers
data.description:
Introduction

Closed-end funds (CEFS) might not get the spotlight like mutual funds or ETFs, but they quietly hold $280 billion in assets, offering investors unique advantages. One such fund, Source Capital (NYSE: NYSE:SOR), has been in the game since 1968, giving us over 20 years of tangible performance data instead of theoretical projections. So, is SOR worth considering? Let's dive into the numbers.

The first CEF started in 1868, and currently, 449 CEFs are openly traded. Source Capital (NYSE: SOR) was incepted in 1968, so we have data for an investment that is longer than 20 years, and the data is not theoretical.

Portfolio Construction

FactSet classifies SOR as a large-cap CEF, but a closer look suggests it operates more like an asset allocation fund:

  • Focus on global large-cap equities
  • Invest in fixed income when no opportunities for equities exist
  • Investments are based on fundamentals with a focus on risk management.

These are the ten largest holdings in SOR’s portfolio and their allocation as of May 31, 2025. Unlike many funds, SOR avoids heavy concentration in single stocks, which helps mitigate volatility.

According to First Pacific Advisors, up to 70% of its holdings should be equities, yet as of May 31, 2025, 58% of its assets are in credit and fixed income, meaning its actual allocation leans more defensive than its stated intent.

SOR has averaged 10.68% annual returns since inception, earning a 5-star rating from Morningstar. However, rating agencies provide no additional commentary. So, let’s break down the numbers:

If one wants a fund that will allocate their investment across several asset classes and geographies, then SOR is worth consideration. Comparing it to SPY, it does just as well as SPY for the likelihood of generating a profit. (0.48 v. 0.48). It outperforms SPY at beating inflation (0.46 v. 0.42) and beating the risk-free rate (0.49 v. 0.46). It does not do as well at controlling downside deviation (0.57 v. 0.65), but it does have a better risk-adjusted performance (10.05% v. 9.46%). Based on risk analysis versus SPY, this fund deserves a rating of Buy at best.

Since this is an allocation fund, it is more appropriate to compare the fund’s performance to a 70/30 or 60/40 allocation. So, we will add to Vanguard Total Bond ETF (XNAS: BND) to SPY to create a comparable mix. Here are the results with my favorite risk metrics.

The simple allocation portfolios outperform SOR in almost every risk category. So, what is the problem if SOR has a better overall return? The problems stem from its rather high standard deviation (19.07%). Since this measure is used as a divisor for all of the risk measures, it lowers the results. At this point, it becomes a question of whether an investor is willing to stomach the sharper ups and downs this fund will have compared to its benchmarks.

My Take

Approaching SOR as a large-cap ETF like FactSet does will cause some confusion, which is why it is important to look at the underlying assets. Without doing so, one might be inclined to view SOR as a 3.5-Star fund. Its overperformance compared to the S&P 500 is appealing, even though the large deviation spread does cause concern.

So, how does one look at SOR? On average, it does minimize losses during down years (3.20% vs. -15.38%). That is an excellent result, but there is a rub. The worst 12-month period for SOR saw a loss of -55.13% (12 months ending 11/2008). That is a tremendous loss of capital for one to stomach for a moderately allocated portfolio, especially when the more traditional 70/30 or 60/40 portfolios limited their losses to half of that amount. On the same token, SOR saw a gain of 98.89% for the 12 months ending in March 2010. While one can say that was during a unique period in stock market history, these results are still part of SOR’s DNA.

SOR offers a compelling balance: strong returns, inflation resistance, and global diversification. However, its high standard deviation (19.07%) means investors must stomach sharper ups and downs compared to traditional allocation portfolios. If you prioritize growth and beating inflation, SOR is a solid pick. If minimizing volatility is your focus, a traditional 70/30 or 60/40 mix might be preferable.

Have fun and go make some money.

data.title: Duolingo: Playing Chess While Others Play Checkers
data.description:
Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL) has been a core holding of mine for some time yet I regretfully stated investors shouldn’t rush to add shares of Duolingo in my last article as I stated the company's valuation seemed lofty. Since that article, Duolingo has crushed the S&P 500 as it’s up by over 40%. YTD Duolingo is up over 60% while the S&P 500 is just barely positive.

In Q1 2025, Duolingo delivered yet another masterful quarter firmly illustrating that naysayers who think AI can displace this company, continue to be absolutely wrong.

Let’s dig into the most recent quarter as I discuss why despite having a high valuation, quality, high performing companies, like Duolingo, aren’t cheap for a reason.

Duo is Far From Dead

When a company’s CEO doesn’t even have prepared remarks, drops a joke, then just goes to Q&A, I think that’s a clear sign the quarter went well.

As it relates to user metrics, in Q1 2025 Duolingo had 46.6 million daily active users (DAUs) which is an increase of 49% year-over-year. Monthly active users came in at 130.2 million which is a 33% increase compared to Q1 2024. Paid subscribers were 10.3 million for the quarter which is an increase of 40% compared to the prior year first quarter. Clearly, these are impressive numbers that illustrate the power of the platform.

Part of the reason the company was able to keep this sustained growth and keep users engaged this quarter was with a very successful marketing campaign in which Duolingo’s mascot Duo the owl died (but thankfully came back to life). This campaign generated over 1.7 billion impressions for the company and as CEO Luis von Ahn stated on the earnings call, the cost of this campaign was “essentially nothing.”

In the quarter, Duolingo also launched chess which will also likely bring new users into the platform and continue to keep current users engaged. Impressively, Duolingo’s chess was first pitched by two employees (non-programmers even) who created a prototype with the help of AI. Here’s what von Ahn had to say about this new addition, “We think there is a huge demand for chess. There are hundreds of millions of people who want to play chess. And the same is true for math and music. I mean they’re growing really well. We are monetizing all of these subjects, by the way. The way you monetize them is the same way we monetize language learning, which is if you want to turn off ads, you can pay to subscribe. So we’re monetizing them already. But compared to language learning, they’re a lot smaller. But yes, we’re very excited about it.”

Clearly this is a positive for Duolingo and it shows the company can continue to add more subjects and categories outside of language learning. With the help of AI I believe Duolingo can continue to roll out more and more subjects in a similar fashion.

Financials

In Q1 2025 Duolingo had revenue of roughly $231 million, which is a 38% increase compared to Q1 2024. Total bookings were roughly $271 million, an increase of 38% compared to the prior year first quarter. Subscription bookings were roughly $232 million, which is a 40% increase compared to Q1 2024.

This revenue growth led to Duolingo posting net income of $35 million and earnings per share of basic share of common stock of $0.78, both notable improvements compared to the prior year as you can see below:

Duolingo is expecting bookings in the range of $243 to $247 million for Q2 2025 and revenue in the range of $238 million to $242 million. For fiscal year 2025, Duolingo expects bookings to be in the range of $1,117 million to $1,126 million. Revenue for the year is expected to be between $987 to $996 million, a year-over year of more than 30%. This illustrates Duolingo expects their strong bookings and revenue growth to continue.

Duolingo has maintained an excellent balance sheet with more than $883 million in cash and cash equivalents. Duolingo has more than enough current assets to cover all of their liabilities (most of which is deferred revenue) and the company has no long-term debt

Finally, from acash flow perspective in Q1 2025 cash flow from operating activities was roughly $106 million and free cash flow from roughly $103 million. This is compared to nearly $84 million of cash flow from operating activities and nearly $79 million in free cash flow in the prior year quarter.

Clearly, Duolingo continues to grow revenue at a stellar pace while increasing net income and cash flows while maintaining no long-term debt. Aside from some dilution due an increase in common stock, investors don’t have too much to complain about after this fantastic quarter.

No Stopping AI

Luis von Ahn has made it clear AI will continue to be a key part of Duolingo's future. The company’s CEO and co-founder made some arguably controversial comments recently about AI speaking about his company and education in general. First von Ahn stated he thought AI could replace contract workers within Duolingo, although he later walked back those comments. Next, von Ahn noted he believed AI was going to be a key part of education in schools. Although, he noted teachers will still be necessary as parents need childcare. I don’t disagree that teachers won’t easily be replaced as they play a key and important role in our communities. However, teaching is a very difficult job with changing curriculum, expanding classroom sizes, and challenging students. Teachers need help and that’s where AI can come in. I believe AI can help cater to specific students' needs and provide teachers with useful insights.

As it relates to Duolingo specifically, von Ahn and team are using AI and they seem to be using it well according to the CEO as on the latest earnings call he stated, “...AI is very transformative for our business. It helps us teach better. It helps us create content a lot faster. It helps us create content that was just infeasible to do before. I mean a good example is we just released 148 language courses. We wouldn’t have done that if it wasn’t for AI. We are really going all in as a company on AI, because it’s a technology that particularly applies to us, because the main improvement in AI in the last couple of years is large language models and language is what we teach. So, it particularly applies to us. So, you will see us – I mean we really are – a lot of features are being developed that are related to AI, and you will see us develop the right features for the users. So, we really want to stay ahead with it. And we believe that we are far ahead of everybody else on this.”

As von Ahn noted, it’s quite impressive that with the use of AI Duolingo was able to create 148 new courses within the span of a year. It took Duolingo 12 years to release 100 courses so it’s quite the feat that 148 were created in a single year. I imagine it will only get easier for Duolingo to continue this pace with the help of AI.

Max has continued to add features with the help of AI as well. For example, in video calls with Lily Duolingo is making Lily’s environment more “realistic” and they are working to create a more immersive, engaging experience. I think these are the type of features that will keep Max users engaged and likely get more users to convert. Plus, as von Ahn noted on the earnings call, the price of Max will come down in certain parts of the world (such as India) which will lead to continued growth in Max users.

Regarding risks, those with a bearish view of Duolingo typically feel AI is the biggest risk to Duolingo. Some feel ChatGPT and similar tools like Google’s Gemini may impact Duolingo’s business. As I’ve stated in prior articles, I don’t see ChatGPT as a direct threat to Duolingo because I think there is a difference between wanting to learn a language and wanting a translation.

I think the bigger threat to Duolingo is a competitor catching up to Duolingo with the help of AI. As stated in Duolingo’s most recent 10K filing, they view competition as one of their biggest risks, stating, “The online language learning industry is highly competitive, with low switching costs and a consistent stream of new products and entrants, and innovation by our competitors may disrupt our business.”

However, I think Duolingo has a first mover advantage in language learning, similar to Netflix’s first mover streaming advantage. Within their 10K, Duolingo’s biggest risk, which I agree with, is keeping existing customers engaged. To me that translates to Duolingo continuing to leverage AI and use it more effectively than their competition. Currently, I do believe Duolingo is doing that and von Ahn and team seem focused on continuing to heavily use AI in the future. Duolingo was never intended to have a model in which humans spoke with other humans. They have also had a tech first mindset which is why Duolingo has the best course content with the most engaging experience and this in turn has led to Duolingo continuing to have the most users within this niche industry.

Valuation

As you can see from the below valuation metrics, Seeking Alpha's overall value grade for Duolingo is a "F"

Although Duolingo is profitability I still believe price to sales is likely the best valuation metric for this growing company.The company's forward price to sales is 24.02 which is still certainly high and much higher than the sector median and if you look at the below graph you'll see Duolingo is at their highest prices to sales ratio in a year:

Certainly, that's a high multiple however the above chart was very similar to the one I posted back in December 2024. Premium companies garner premium valuations and like acclaimed investor and author Chris Mayer said, "Great stocks have a ready fan club, and many will spend of their time near 52-week highs, as you'd expect. It is rare to get a truly great business at dirt-cheap prices."

If you're paying for quality then you're paying up but in the long run it's worth it. In the case of top-notch companies like Duolingo, investors can dollar-cost average into a position without worrying about buying near all-time highs.

In looking at Duolingo's valuation from another viewpoint, I updated my reverse discounted cash flow model and with a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3% you would need a growth rate of 26% to justify Duolingo’s current share price:

Now that's certainly a high growth rate but current analysts are estimating Duolingo's growth will meet if not exceed 26% in the next three years. Furthermore if you envision Duolingo continuing to launch new non-language courses like what the company just did with chess then I certainly think it's feasible Duolingo can continue to grow at these high rates as they launch then monetize new courses.

Conclusion

Duolingo delivered another stellar quarter as revenue, net income, MAUs, DAUs and paid subscribers all continued to grow.

I believe Duolingo’s moat within the language learning space is clearly intact and Duolingo’s use of AI seems far ahead of competitors. I don’t believe ChatGPT or similar artificial intelligence applications will impact Duolingo’s business.

Some would probably view Duolingo as an expensive stock yet I firmly believe premium businesses justify premium valuations and Duolingo has proven quarter after quarter this an elite business with much more growth coming in the years to come.

This is a business long-term investors can dollar cost average into and feel confident that over the years to come, Duolingo will continue to be an excellent investment for growth oriented investors.

data.title: FormFactor: The AI Thesis Has Yet To Deliver
data.description:
FormFactor (NASDAQ:FORM), a supplier of test and measuring solutions for the semiconductor industry, has continued to surrender some of its prior gains, which were accumulated thanks to FORM being perceived as a beneficiary of the boom in AI. The stock is now available for about half of what it went for at the peak in mid-2024 after selling off for much of 2025, but it may still be too early to be thinking of long FORM. The stock arguably does not, among other things, offer good enough value, at least not yet. Why will be covered next.

FORM may be caught in a downtrend

A previous article from October 2024 mentioned how FORM had greatly benefited from in essence becoming a play on artificial intelligence or AI due to being a supplier of equipment needed to produce HBM memory, which in turn is used along with GPUs for the training and inference AI requires. However, the article noted that this exposure to AI can cut both ways by pushing the stock higher, but also lower if the AI story does not play out the way expected. In the end, the article decided to rate FORM a hold.

The chart above shows how the stock has moved lower in the nine months that have passed. FORM peaked at $63.63 in July 2024, but the stock has moved lower since then with a series of lower highs, which are connected to form a descending upper trendline in the chart. The overall direction of the stock appears to be down based on this, which may discourage potential buyers of the stock.

More significantly, the stock is approaching the upper trendline. The significance of this is that whenever the stock came close to or into contact with this trendline, the stock proceeded to turn lower. This is what has happened in the last 1-2 months, but also December/January and July of last year, as shown in the chart. This suggests FORM needs to be treated with caution because a move downwards in the stock may be close at hand due to resistance, in line with the existing trend.

It’s also worth mentioning that the July 2024 high of $63.63 was the culmination of a long move upwards in the stock that started with a low of $18.15 in November 2022. The stock’s most recent low of $22.58 in early April came close, but did not quite reach the November 2022 low of $18.15. The stock has moved higher since early April, but if the stock wants to complete a 100% Fibonacci retracement of the prior move upwards with a move downwards, then the bottom may not yet be in. FORM could still be heading lower due to this.

Is FORM still an AI play?

FORM supplies probe cards and other products to more than just the DRAM industry, but it is the DRAM segment that has been the star performer for FORM, especially in recent quarters. This growth in the DRAM segment has been assisted by the ongoing industry transition towards DDR5 and LPDDR5, but the biggest driver of growth has been HBM memory, which is needed for AI applications.

Demand for HBM memory has been so strong that it can be said to be in short supply. DRAM manufacturers have been raising production to meet this demand, which in turn has benefited FORM because of the testing HBM requires. HBM is also much more test intensive than say regular DRAM for PCs or mobile devices, which further increases growth opportunities for FORM.

FORM can therefore still be seen as a play on the boom in AI, which is still going strong. With that said, there is no denying the decline in the stock in the past 12 months, especially in 2025, which was caused by several factors. FORM, for instance, has been affected by the worldwide tariff dispute, like other stocks. While FORM does most manufacturing in the U.S., it does import components used in various products, which means tariffs are relevant to FORM.

FORM has also been negatively affected by export controls, which resulted in lost sales to China in recent quarters. This has affected growth, which, combined with relatively high multiples the stock went for due to the long rally in 2022-2024, made it difficult for FORM to keep flying high and the stock had no choice but to move lower in response to the aforementioned headwinds.

Is FORM performing as billed?

Another factor that contributed to the recent decline in the stock is the fact that FORM is in a mini slump of sorts in terms of demand. FORM is supposed to be a growth stock with its exposure to AI, and all its potential use cases, which is the main reason why the stock price was bid up after the AI boom got going, but recent growth has struggled to move forward.

In the most recent report or Q1 FY2025 report, for instance, revenue declined sequentially for the second consecutive quarter to $171.4M, which resulted in GAAP EPS of $0.08 and non-GAAP EPS of $0.23. In comparison, FORM earned $0.28 and $0.19, respectively, on revenue of $168.7M in Q1 FY2024.

Guidance calls for revenue of $185-195M, GAAP EPS of $0.14-0.22 and non-GAAP EPS of $0.26-0.34 in Q2 FY2025. In comparison, FORM earned $0.25 and $0.35, respectively, on revenue of $197.5M in Q2 FY2024. In other words, growth has not exactly been roaring forward, but more on the flattish side and this has been the case for some time.

Earlier in the year FORM closed the books on FY2024 by reporting revenue of $764M, which is not much different from three years ago or FY2021 when revenue came in at $770M. What this shows is that AI has not been that much of a game changer thus far for FORM. AI-related revenue has definitely had an impact, specifically in terms of revenue related to HBM, but this has been offset by weakness elsewhere, in NAND and other segments, which resulted in flattish growth.

In fact, had it not been for HBM, recent results could have been significantly worse. All the growth in the current quarter, for instance, is expected to be due to HBM. From the Q1 FY2025 earnings call:

“really all the growth in DRAM is coming from HBM. We see the non-HBM, so DDR5, low power DDR5, a little bit of residual DDR4 continuing to be pretty steady, but flat at around $20 million a quarter, which historically for form factor is a cyclical low for the commodity DRAM business.

So, really, all the sequential growth Q1 to Q2 that we're projecting is due to HBM growth.”

Source: FORM earnings call

With the exception of HBM, the rest of the market is not that strong for FORM. The issue here is that FORM supplies products for more than just HBM, and if it is to grow at the pace the market would like it to, all segments need to perform well and contribute, not just one.

Is FORM worth buying at current levels?

FORM is available at less than half the asking price at the peak in mid-2024. Yet there is an argument to be made that the stock is still not worth buying despite the sizable price cut. According to the methodology favored by people like Peter Lynch, a stock needs to have a PEG ratio below one in order to be considered undervalued relative to expected growth, but it appears FORM does not meet this criteria.

Non-GAAP EPS reached $1.20 TTM, which confers FORM a P/E ratio of 26.1x with the stock closing at $31.36 on June 3. So in order for PEG to come in at less than one, FORM needs to grow earnings by more than 26.1% per year, on average, in the next five years. This looks like a difficult task because FORM has not managed to grow at that kind of pace.

Non-GAAP EPS reached $1.15 in the last completed fiscal or FY2024, up from $1.04 in FY2019, which means EPS grew at a CAGR of just 2% in FY2019-2024, or well below the more than 26.1% needed. If earnings grow by just 2% per year, on average, in the next five years or FY2024-2029, you get a PEG far above one. This suggests FORM is probably overvalued relative to the earnings growth it is likely to achieve.

Moreover, while it is not impossible for FORM to grow earnings at a faster pace in the coming years than in the past several years, it appears that even FORM does not think it can achieve a very high growth rate. The latest financial model presented by FORM targets non-GAAP EPS of $2.00. FORM did not state the exact timeframe FORM wants to achieve this target, but assuming the model refers to the next five years and FY2024 as the base, then EPS would need to grow at a CAGR of 11.7% in FY2024-2029. This is better than before, but not enough, because it implies a PEG ratio of well above one, which suggests FORM is still not a buy at current levels.

Investor takeaways

The halving of the stock price in the past 12 months can be attributed to several reasons. Export controls have resulted in lost sales in China. The global dispute about tariffs have not left FORM unscathed. Recent quarterly results have not lived up to FORM’s billing as a growth stock thanks to AI, which is problematic due to the relatively high multiples the stock is assigned. FORM still trades at 26x TTM earnings, even after the big reduction in the stock price.

FORM has certainly benefited from the strong demand for HBM memory, but other segments have not been as strong and DRAM, let alone HBM, revenue only makes up a minority of sales. This weakness in other segments has negated the growth due to HBM, which has resulted in flattish growth at FORM,. The flat results stand in contrast to FORM’s billing as a growth stock driven by AI demand, which is why the stock price was bid up in the first place.

The stock price has been cut in half, but if people like Peter Lynch are right, FORM still does not offer good value. FORM needs to grow EPS at a relatively fast pace of more than 26.1% per year in the next several years to offset the relatively high P/E ratio assigned to FORM and in order for PEG to come in at less than one, but there is reason to doubt if FORM can achieve this desired growth rate.

If FORM grows earnings in the coming years at the same pace it did in recent years, then FORM looks overvalued. FORM needs to do much better than it has done in recent years, but it seems that even FORM does not think it can grow earnings at a CAGR of more than 26.1% in order to get a PEG ratio of less than one. The financial model suggests a CAGR of no more than the mid teens is achievable. So even if FORM hits the targets set out in the current financial model, which is not guaranteed, FORM would still end up with a PEG of 2.2x. It can therefore be argued that FORM does not offer good enough value to be worth buying, despite the recent decrease in the price of the stock.

I am neutral on FORM with the above in mind. If FORM is to become a buy it needs to offer good enough value to entice buyers to step in. This requires much faster earnings growth, or, alternatively, if this is too difficult as suggested by the current financial model, a further reduction in the stock price. If the stock does head lower, which the charts suggest is a real possibility, then FORM needs to be re-evaluated. At this time, FORM is not worth a buy.

data.title: NAC: It's Difficult To Grasp The Final Strategy Here
data.description:
If we know the structure then we can divine the purpose

There are many things that it is possible to do through the manipulation - perhaps design is a lighter word - of the structure of a financial instrument. Different situations can be turned into appeals to different financial interests and so on. We can turn an income stream into capital and vice versa and so on. So, it pays us to understand what an instrument is doing. That's the way we can work out whether the instrument is doing what we want to have done.

Nuveen California Quality Municipal Income Fund (NYSE:NAC)

This is a bond fund investing in California municipals. This means the income will be income tax free for California residents. So, that's the first thing we can say here, this is aimed at California income tax payers. If you're not in California, or earn too little to be paying income tax - and especially if you're a foreigner outside the country - then this just isn't aimed at you.

Bonds

Bonds are lending to someone but with a tradeable piece of paper rather than just a direct loan. Bonds then come in three major flavours.

Treasuries are lending to the Federal government. It's not true that national governments never go bust but we all still also accept that the US Treasury is credit risk free. Partly on the grounds that it hasn't gone bust for a couple of centuries now, partly on the grounds that it controls the printing press for money and majorly because, well, there's got to be some base ground for what we consider risk. If the Treasury goes then we've all got much bigger problems than financial markets anyway.

So, Treasuries are said at least to be free of credit risk. But all bonds are subject to interest rate risk. If interest rates change then old bonds will be worth a different amount than new.

Just ponder for a moment. Here's a piece of paper that says 4% on $100 for 10 years. Here's another that says 5% on $100 for 10 years. The two are worth different amounts of money, obviously. The difference being the net present value of that extra $1 a year for 10 years. So, bonds already in issue change value relative to newly issued bonds when underlying interest rates change. This is known as credit risk. It works going up and down as well.

Clearly, we are investing in bonds in order to gain a return. But, just one of those things, longer bonds pay higher interest rates than shorter. We can put that the other way around and still be right too. Longer bonds have greater interest rate risk therefore they pay a higher coupon - we get paid for risk in financial markets.

Corporates are lending to companies and we know that companies can go bust. Therefore we here face credit risk as well as interest rate risk. As we're taking more risk we should be paid a higher coupon. We do - it's very rare, even though it does at times happen, that a company can borrow cheaper than the Feds.

The way out of credit risk is to have a diversified portfolio. Sure, sure, one investment can go bust but without all our eggs in one basket that won't be too painful. And, if we've been sensible, then the higher coupon on all investments will make up for the occasional failure. More than make up in fact.

We can buy corporate - and other - bonds in $1,000 size but usually only at issuance and sell at redemption. For corporate bonds the secondary market can insist upon much larger minimum lot sizes. The European corporate bond markets tend to be minimum $200k par value lot size for example. This means that trying to build an actively managed and diversified portfolio in bonds can be hugely expensive - or, perhaps not expensive, but require lots and lots of money. The solution to this for near all of us is to be in a bond fund of some kind.

The managers of the bond fund do the diversification in size and then we all own a fraction of that fund and gain the diversification that way.

Municipals are lending to sub-national government organisations within the US. They face both interest rate risk and credit risk. so, the same trade offs about long and short term, coupons paid and also the need for diversification - meaning, again, likely the use of a fund.

Munis are in theory as subject to credit risk as corporates. But default rates are really pretty low - 0.10% is one recent estimate. So, munis should pay, on that combined risk basis, more than Treasuries and the same or less than Corporates. They don't - they pay lower coupons than Treasuries.

The reason is that the income from a muni bond is free of income tax.

Tax equivalent yields

This then means that we cannot, not really, compare the yields of munis to Treasuries and corporates. What we want to know is how much will we end up with in our hand from each? Or, as we actually calculate it, the other way around. What yield would we need to gain from a taxable investment in order to equal that one from a non-taxable? This is called the tax equivalent yield.

Obviously this depends upon the tax rate of the recipient of the income. So it's not a wholly easy calculation.

It's also true that munis are free of the Federal income tax. But only those bonds issued in state are also free of the state income tax if there happens to be one. Thus we end up with funds specialising in tax free incomes for inhabitants of specific states. BNY for New Yorkers for example. VTEC in California. Or this, NAC, for Californians.

Fortunately people publish tables of this for us:

As we can see at higher incomes this tax-e yield really makes a difference. At low incomes, below the tax system, it makes none. The usual rule of thumb here is that it all starts to makes sense at a marginal tax rate of 24%. The tax free offsets the lower coupon at about that rate. Given the addition of the CA income tax here that might run a little lower.

So, our first set of conclusions

A fund in California tax free municipal bonds might make sense for a resident of California on 24% or higher Federal income tax rates. At less than probably not worth the lower coupon. This does assume that investing is being done for income, not capital gains, reasons. It's also not worth it if the investing is being done inside a tax protected wrapper - 401k and the like. There's just no point in that double layer of tax protection - if we're getting the protection anyway then the tax-e yield doesn't work.

So, if you're in those higher tax brackets, living in California, looking for income, this might be for you.

The usual fund details

This is a closed-end fund - that means that when we buy we are buying stock off a selling investor, not adding money into the fund. This also means the fund cannot suffer a redemption cascade - where we all turn up at the same time looking for our money back. That can lead to a fire sale of assets and thus significant losses. The risks of a run rise with the illiquidity of the underlying investments. There have been at least two commercial property open-ended funds that got into real difficulties over this. On the other hand, a fund in Treasuries doesn't have to worry about it. Mid-tenor municipals? Something to note but not worry greatly about, as here.

On the other hand a closed end fund can leverage up. Most do, to about 40%. 41.66% effective leverage here. The point being that the incoming coupons are 4.23%, the cost of leverage is 3.82%, so a modest boost to investor returns. At, obviously, a - small - risk if bond prices wholly collapse.

The other side of a closed-end fund is that it can stray from net asset value:

The financials are:

At 339 holdings it's a fairly selective fund. The universe of California mutuals is very much larger (VTEC is in 2,600) so NAC is definitely not trying to just hold the index.

As we can also see a fairly long tenor (maturity) there. So, looking to invest long for coupon but at that interest rate risk of capital value changes. Do note that there's no "answer" to this. It's simply a trade off that has to be made.

Management fees - at 0.93% - aren't out of line with actively managed funds.

We can also see the major holdings:

Has to be said that 4% of a fund in just one position is fairly chunky. But that's what the management fee is for, that someone makes those decisions for us.

All in all it's pretty much what it says on the can. Fairly standard closed end fund investing in tax free municipals with a special emphasis on benefiting stockholders in California. Management fees, leverage, all the usuals, roughly in line with the usual norms. So, what's not to like?

The bit that worries is this

The final decision making part of such a fund is that of the payout. And here:

Distributions are currently estimated to include the following amounts from sources other than net investment income: 0% capital gains and 48% return of capital.

Umm, OK. The market distribution rate, as we've seen already, is 8.08%. But pretty much half of that is coming from return of capital? Not coupon within the fund at all?

Now there's nothing wrong with that. It's a strategy, just like any other. But we do need - as up at the top - to grasp the strategy to see whether it accords with our desires. And it's, well to me at least, difficult to see who this particular strategy suits.

Now it is possible to be cynical here. Assume that not everyone delves as deep into the construction of an instrument as we are doing here. They invest, rather, on slightly more headline numbers - like that fund yield rather than where it's coming from. Thus a fund manager might push up the yield - say through capital return - in order to gain more traffic. Could be, could be.

But we who are digging deeper, well, is that something we actually want? After all, our capital is ours anyway so why would we want to have a tax free fund returning our capital to us as a tax privileged income? What's the point of this? We could be in some other fund - tax privileged or not - and simply sell some portion of our holding each year with no tax implications at all and thereby gain capital return.

Which is where I end up.

Why I'm wrong

There could be some good reason why a tax privileged bond fund returns capital as income. I can't see it myself but I'm always open to the idea that my not understanding something is about me not the world. That's certainly true of certain parts of macroeconomics for example, or the appeal of Ye's music.

But without that reason then I'm not wrong. The description of the fund is as it is. The appeal of the fund - to income taxpaying Californians - is as it is.

My opinion

I do grasp that those looking for income might look at the rate of income and not wholly and fully at the source of it. The idea of a tax-privileged bond fund returning over 8% is great. When we move to a tax-e yield, and considering California's top income tax rates, we're looking at something like 16% as a yield. Which is really great in something as low risk as muni bonds.

But near that half of the income is coming as capital return. Which is where I cannot see the point. The universe of high income - therefore high tax rate - Californians who are also looking to turn capital into income seems to me to be likely to be pretty small.

NAC is definitely only for California state income tax payers. That's in the base design. But that choice to boost the yield with capital returns? That makes it only for higher rate CA taxpayers who want to convert capital into and income - not gain an income from capital, but convert. If you are one of those and that's what you want to do then why not. But I'd suggest that somewhere north of 99% of the people who read Seeking Alpha are not both. Therefore, well, don't.

data.title: Plains All American: Very Attractive Yield Following Sell-Off
data.description:
Article Thesis

Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. (PAA) (PAGP) is an energy midstream company that has seen its shares come under pressure recently, which is why its dividend yield has risen to a very attractive level of 9%.

Midstream Coverage

I have not written about Plains All American in the past, but I have covered many other energy midstream payers here on Seeking Alpha. If you are interested, you can check out my articles on Enbridge (ENB), Energy Transfer (ET), and so on. With PAA having seen its shares drop by more than 10% over the last couple of months, which made its yield climb to 9%, it is time to add it to my energy midstream coverage universe today.

Company Overview

Plains All American Pipeline is, as the name suggests, an energy infrastructure player that is focused on pipelines. While not among the largest companies in its industry, Plains All American is sizable, nevertheless, owning assets across much of the US and some parts of Canada:

The company owns crude oil and natural gas liquids pipelines, with the former being the much bigger and more important segment that also generates the majority of PAA's profits and cash flow, with around 85% of company-wide EBITDA being generated in the crude oil segment. The following chart shows the geographic breakdown of PAA's crude oil business, which includes pipelines as well as gathering:

We see that PAA is heavily exposed to the Permian Basin, which is something that I like -- the Permian is the biggest and most relevant oil play in the US. Other, smaller oil plays round out PAA's footprint, but these are less important for the company overall. On the natural gas liquids side, PAA's assets include fractionation, gathering, storage, and pipelines, with around half of the company's EBITDA from this segment being fee-based and thus relatively immune to commodity price swings. Some of the revenues that are not fee-based are hedged, thereby reducing the company's commodity price exposure further, which is why, on a company-wide basis, commodity exposure is pretty slim -- around 80% of cash flows are either hedged or fee-based, leaving just 20% of cash flows exposed to ups and downs in oil and NGL markets. This, of course, means that PAA does not benefit a lot when commodity prices explode upwards, unlike, for example, an E&P company. But on the other hand, PAA is not exposed a lot during times of weaker commodity prices, such as right now. Currently, crude oil prices are weak, due to the market worrying that trade tensions and tariffs could cause a recession. Upstream energy companies feel a substantial impact, but PAA is not exposed a lot, making PAA a lower-risk, more resilient company in the broad energy space (this also holds true for PAA's peers with a lot of fee-based cash flows).

Plains All American is trading with a somewhat complicated structure, as there is more than one entity. PAA is a limited partnership where investors get a K-1, which can, depending on one's circumstances, be a pro or a con. But investors can also invest in PAGP, or Plains GP Holdings, which owns a sizable position in PAA and which is taxed as a C-corp, and where investors get a comparable dividend of $1.52 per year. For those who do not want a K-1, PAGP is thus a viable alternative, although it is slightly pricier than PAA.

Looking at Plains All American's track record, we see the following:

Over the last couple of years, things moved in the right direction -- adjusted EBITDA, with one-time items being excluded, rose substantially and reliably, while cash generation was strong as well. The company used some of its cash flow to pay down debt, which, in combination with rising EBITDA, made its leverage ratio (net debt divided by EBITDA) drop meaningfully. But while PAA was able to deleverage its balance sheet, it still had ample surplus cash flows, which is why investors benefited from highly compelling distribution growth on top of that, which is one of the key reasons why I believe that PAA is attractive right here.

PAA: A High Yield With Strong Distribution Growth

Since 2021, Plains All American has increased its distribution at a rate of 21% per year on average, which makes for a combined increase of around 110%, which is, I believe, very attractive. At the beginning of the year, Plains All American hiked its dividend by 19.7%, which is almost in line with the growth rate over the last couple of years. A high dividend growth rate is positive, but not a reason to buy all by itself, of course. But in Plains All American's case, investors also get a very nice starting dividend yield, and dividend safety isn't an issue, I believe. With shares trading around $16.80 right now, PAA offers a dividend yield of 9.05% -- an extremely high dividend yield relative to the broad market, and also one of the best yields compared to what is available from major energy midstream companies:

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer, MPLX (MPLX), Kinder Morgan (KMI), Kinetik (KNTK), and TC Energy (TRP) are some of the major midstream companies investors can pick from -- they all offer very solid to strong yields, but PAA offers a higher yield than all of them.

The company guides for strong distribution growth in the coming years, too -- PAA wants to increase its payout by $0.15 per year starting in 2026, following the $0.25 increase this year. While KMI shareholders will know that management's goals do not always come to fruition, I think that PAA's goal is not unrealistic for the foreseeable future. After all, the company has a pretty strong balance sheet today, thus there is little need to further bring down debt levels, and distribution coverage looks quite strong right now, with 2025 distributable cash flow being expected at $1.875 billion, which results in a 175% coverage ratio. If PAA were to increase its distribution by $0.15 next year, the payout would climb to $1.67, for ~10% annual growth. The distribution coverage ratio would be 160% in this scenario if PAA does not grow its distributable cash flow at all over the next year, but that seems quite unlikely. Between fee increases and rising volumes on existing pipelines, the buildout of new pipelines, and tuck-in acquisitions, PAA has delivered nice growth in the last couple of years, and I expect the same going forward.

While PAA won't be able to grow its distribution by 20% per year as it did over the last couple of years, there should still be meaningful distribution growth. In combination with the high starting yield investors get right now, PAA seems like a nice dividend growth investment. Even if PAA were to grow its distribution by just 5% per year going forward -- which is below management's guidance -- PAA could be a strong income investment. The payout would climb to close to $2.00 by 2030, for a yield on cost of around 12%. If distributions are reinvested at an average yield of 9% over the next five years, the yield on cost could climb to 18%. This example shows that the combination of a high starting yield and some growth can be quite compelling for those with a longer-term investment horizon.

Is PAA Attractive Here?

As shown above, PAA aims for $1.875 billion in distributable cash flow this year. This means that the company trades at just 6.3x its distributable cash flow, based on a market capitalization of around $12 billion, which makes for a DCF yield of around 16%. This could be very appropriate for a low-growth company with massive debt, but for a company like PAA, the current valuation feels too low -- it has grown reliably in the past and its balance sheet is now pretty clean, which is why I believe that the company deserves a higher valuation.

No company is risk-free, but commodity price movements are only a small risk for PAA thanks to many fee-based contracts and PAA's hedges. Regulatory risks also seem low under the current administration, I believe. All in all, PAA offers a very strong yield at a pretty low valuation, while investors can expect both company growth and distribution growth over the coming years. PAA has dropped from around $21 at the beginning of the year, despite the business outlook being more or less the same, which to me looks like a buying opportunity.

data.title: 2 BDCs With 20%+ Discounts, 1 To Buy And 1 To Avoid
data.description:
I have been covering the BDC (publicly traded private credit) space on the Seeking Alpha platform quite extensively for the past ~2 years.

In this period, the sector has experienced an interesting ride.

The things got going for BDCs really since late mid 2021, when there was a combination of depressed banking activity and rising inflation. The banks sitting on the sidelines resulted in an increased demand for alternative lending, while rising inflations introduced an expectation about having higher base rates in the near future. Since most BDCs issue floating credit, higher base rates tend to render a direct positive hit to the BDC earnings profiles. On top of that, all of this created a huge uncertainty in the system, leading to elevated spreads, which are very important for BDC value creation as well (i.e., it is SOFR + spread, which forms a total yield that BDCs can charge to their "customers").

In the 2021–2024 period, there were obviously some ups and downs, but, in general, it was a feast time for BDCs and their investors.

However, in the mid-2024, we could already observe some signs of weakness emerging. Just as the sector experienced a positive boost from the expectations of higher base rates in mid-2021, in 2024 the process was reversed. Namely, the expectations of gradually falling interest rates had a direct negative hit to the BDC valuation multiples in 2024.

Plus, as it is usually the case, whenever there is a specific niche that is booming, there is automatically a huge amount of fresh capital forming. Namely, the attractive returns realized by BDCs caught asset managers' attention, causing a significant capital inflow into the private credit space. This, in turn, has led to a notable decline in spreads (i.e., the classical supply and demand issue).

As a result of depressed spreads and lower base rates, the overall BDC sector is in a quite fragile position. The margin of safety in the sector is almost gone, and many BDCs carry very tiny dividend coverage levels.

This has opened up rather heavy discounts in the sector. For example, the median sector-level P/NAV discount is 9%.

Obviously, the median value captures the middle spot in the BDC P/NAV list, and, in reality, there are quite many players that have even more pronounced discounts.

So, in this article, I would like to elaborate on two 20%+ discounted BDCs. In one case the discount is fair or potentially not as aggressive as it should be, but in the other case, in my view, investors have an opportunity to pick up an unfairly punished BDC.

To avoid: TPVG

TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) is a BDC, which invests in venture capital backed companies. Typically, these companies have not yet achieved organic cash flow neutrality and are still in the process of forming a clear strategy and market penetration tactics to actually turn the investment thesis into a healthy cash flow case.

What this means is that TPVG investors assume two considerable risk positions:

  1. BDC-specific risks which are relevant for all sector participants (and the baseline risks here are already quite high).
  2. Venture capital company risks that are associated with notable probabilities of corporate / business plan failures.

One of the best ways to mitigate the first risk position is to focus more on high-quality portfolio companies that have robust credit statistics and healthy cash flows. In TPVG's case, the portfolio aspect brings just more headwinds into the equation.

Having said that, we have to appreciate that TPVG trades at a 20% discount to NAV. Plus, it offers one of the highest dividend yields in the sector - almost 17%. These are rather attractive characteristics. Also, let's remember that in 2024, TPVG made a dividend cut, decreasing the quarterly base dividend of $0.40 per share to a new level of $0.30 per share (i.e., theoretically, positive for the current dividend coverage).

However, let me give you three reasons why, in my view, TPVG is far from being a bargain.

First, the Q1, 2025 net investment income per share landed at $0.27, registering a continued trend of a declining income generation. For example, in Q4, 2024, the result was $0.30 per share and the quarter before the NII per share was $0.35. This means three things:

  • The current base dividend coverage is 90% (i.e., the dividend is not covered by the generated cash flows).
  • TPVG has to use debt to cover the gap.
  • The overall trajectory sends a signal that the next quarter will likely result in an even lower NII per share.

Second, speaking of the third bullet, I do believe that Q2, 2025 will indeed bring a lower NII per share result. The main reason here is that TPVG's 14%+ yielding loan book has a yield that is materially above the level of yield that TPVG can access from its new deals. For instance, in Q1, the newly signed transactions had a weighted average yield of 13.3% yield. And a difference of ~100 basis points is really significant. So, there is still some pain left in the portfolio that has to be experienced before the TPVG yields match those that could be obtained in the market.

Third, what I described above does not include any headwinds from the portfolio end, which seems quite unlikely at this moment. Namely, it is hard to imagine how there would not be incremental non-accruals sometime in the near to medium-term, which are inherently negative to the NII per share generation. This is especially a risk for such companies in which TPVG deploys its capital.

Given the above, a 20% discount seems more than fair.

To buy: CCAP

Crescent Capital BDC, Inc. (NASDAQ:CCAP) is a standard BDC, which mostly invests in first-lien loans that rank the highest in company capital structures (i.e., the most lender-friendly place).

Moreover, 99% of CCAP's loan book is backed by private equity sponsors, which significantly de-risk the overall position as the incentive for PEs is to do whatever it takes to successfully monetize their portfolio companies. For example, if there is an additional need for equity to finalize certain CapEx project, which then would generate incremental cash flow, the chances are high that a PE will step in, thereby avoiding covenant breach (or the materialization of credit risk).

What is noteworthy is that CCAP's portfolio company average loan to value is 39%, which is a very conservative level and an indicative of above sector average quality.

However, CCAP trades at a considerable discount, which is actually even steeper than for TPVG. Currently, the P/NAV is 0.78x - a 22% discount.

There is definitely a reason why the market has become worried about CCAP. Yet, there is no reason for so extensive discount.

Let me explain.

A very big driver for the discount that we see now is the NII per share change from Q4, 2024 to Q1, 2025. The NII per share dropped to $0.45 in Q1, 2025 from $0.55 per share in the prior quarter.

It was a combination of several factors that drove down the NII result, but the main one was clearly the emergence of new non-accruals.

The non-accrual base moved up from 0.6% in the prior quarter to 1.8% now. This just shows how sensitive BDC earnings are to even slight changes in the non-accruals. Imagine what would be the effects on TPVG's NII per share if there was, say, a 0.5% uptick of new non-accruals.

The reason why the non-accrual level jumped higher was tied to several unrelated companies experiencing financial difficulties, where in certain instances the potential for a sound recovery remains high.

The management does not expect having new ones, but instead the assumption is that a part of the Q1 non-accruals will come back online.

Having said that, it is definitely worth noting that based on Q1, 2025 NII per share generation, the base dividend is still covered at 107%. In this context, I really liked the comment by the management in the recent earnings call:

This principle guided us to not aggressively raise our base dividend when the rate hiking cycle began in 2022. This marks our 37th consecutive quarter of earning our regular dividend at CCAP, which we have accomplished while maintaining NAV per share within a tight band. We are proud of this track record and are focused on earning our dividend for the foreseeable future. Our view is that dividend yields in the BDC space remain elevated given the current base rate outlook and lack of meaningful fundamental headwinds that have been demonstrated in corporate credit portfolios to date. Our positioning has and always will be for the long term.

Apart from the extraordinary items realized in the Q1 period, the underlying business is robust (e.g., sound portfolio company credit statistics, defensive loan positions, covered dividend, acceptable leverage etc.).

Paying a 22% discount does not make sense to me here.

The bottom line

In this article, I have outlined the key reasons that have made the BDC asset class from an investment of choice to an investment of elevated risk.

I have also elaborated on two BDCs, which from the surface present a compelling opportunity for investors to open long positions. Namely, the opportunity to buy the assets at a 20%+ discount sounds very enticing.

At the same time, I have presented more details, arguing that one of them is a value trap, while the other is actually an investment with a sound risk to reward profile.

data.title: Diamond Hill Large Cap Concentrated Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets were mixed in Q1 as the new presidential administration made repeated waves with an avalanche of activity, which left markets uncertain of the likely path forward. For the quarter, US stocks fell -4.7% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index), notching markets’ first quarterly decline since Q3 2023. Mid-cap stocks held up best, falling a more modest -3.4%, while large caps were down -4.5%. Smaller stocks were hit harder, falling -9.5% — a relatively unsurprising development given smaller companies’ shares are generally more volatile as macroeconomic uncertainty increases. From a style perspective, value fared better up and down the cap spectrum, with large-cap value managing a positive gain of +2.1%. Mid-cap value stocks fell -2.1%, compared to -7.1% for their growth counterparts, while small-cap value stocks declined -7.7% versus -11.1% for small growth (all returns as measured by their respective Russell indices). The shift from growth to value was also relatively unsurprising given the flight to safety prompted by tremendous uncertainty around trade and other fiscal policy shifts in the US.

From a sector perspective, likewise, more defensive sectors in the Russell 1000 Value Index led in the quarter, while cyclical stocks fell out of favor. Energy stocks were the best performers, up +9.5% amid supportive language from President Trump’s administration about its willingness to increase US oil production. Communication services also rose nicely, up +7.0%, as did health care, which rose +6.4% and utilities, which gained +6.2%. Conversely, information technology stocks were hit hardest, declining -5.7%, while consumer discretionary stocks fell -4.5%.

What began in Q4 — market leadership transitions in anticipation of potential winners and losers from a major shift in executive-level policy direction — vigorously continued in Q1. To some extent, economic data took a backseat in the quarter as investors seemed to focus narrowly on the likeliest implications of major trade policy shifts announced during the quarter by President Trump. Shortly after the quarter concluded, Trump announced a raft of tariffs to go into effect immediately on what he dubbed “Liberation Day.” As has been the case since Trump began increasing tariffs, countries are responding in various ways, with some reaching out to the US to negotiate better rates and others — notably, China — choosing to retaliate with their own high tariff rates. Many companies have preemptively announced significant investments in their US operations — ostensibly moves aimed at avoiding tariffs themselves. Ultimately, whether the “Liberation Day” moniker goes down in history or infamy remains to be seen. What is clear is markets are very nervous about the president’s apparent determination to wholesale (and possibly singlehandedly) rework the global trade landscape, even with countries that have historically been significant trade allies, like Taiwan. We’ll have more thoughts on potential direction from here later in this communication.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held rates in March, citing the likelihood tariffs increase inflationary pressures, while cutting its growth expectations for US GDP in 2025. And the US dollar — which one would generally expect to be bolstered relative to other global currencies as tariffs go up (higher prices for US goods increase demand for US dollars with which to pay for them) — counterintuitively fell as tariff language toughened, perhaps signaling investors’ growing concerns tariffs will contribute to a stagflationary US economic environment with high inflation and low or sluggish growth.

Globally, geopolitical uncertainty remains high, though there have been some signs of modest progress toward ending the war between Russia and Ukraine. Following a dramatic fall-out between President Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the US and Ukraine seemed to slowly make their way back toward a minerals and mining deal which would meaningfully increase the US’s economic involvement in Ukraine and potentially pave the way for a broader peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. However, to date, Russian President Vladimir Putin has stymied the US’s attempts to broker a ceasefire between the two countries. War in the Middle East continues, too, as ceasefires between Israel and Hamas and Israel and Lebanon ended and violence across the region re- escalated. The US, too, struck Houthi rebels in Yemen toward the end of the quarter in an attempt to quell attacks on Red Sea shipping lanes.

While volatility is unsettling, it also introduces interesting investing opportunities for those who have been diligent in their research all along. We have no unique insight into how deep this correction will become, whether it will enter bear market territory or how long it will last. However, we know it has helped reset valuations and offered some compelling opportunities to purchase high-quality names at more attractive prices than we’ve seen in some time. Once the trade-related dust settles, time will tell which sectors and individual companies are winners and losers. In the meantime, we will continue deploying our time-tested approach, which is well-built for uncertain market environments like we’re currently seeing — and which we wouldn’t be surprised to see persist for the foreseeable future.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio outperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index in Q1. Relative strength was heavily concentrated among our financials and industrials holdings — the latter delivering a positive Q1 return while benchmark peers were in the red. Conversely, our consumer discretionary holdings were the primary relative headwind.

Among our top individual contributors in Q1 were American International Group (AIG) and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A,BRK.B). Property and casualty insurance company AIG is delivering high-quality results: Its most recently reported underlying loss ratio was better than expected, as were catastrophe losses and prior-year development. Further, the company continues generating revenue growth from both its commercial and consumer segments. We are pleased with the progress AIG is making to streamline its business and capitalize on opportunities for earnings growth better than competitors’. We believe it remains well-positioned to scale revenues efficiently and deliver sustainable value as it continues reducing expenses and returning excess capital via share repurchases.

Diversified holding company Berkshire Hathaway has benefited from improved performance at GEICO, which is generating better underwriting results, policy growth and a lower expense ratio than peers. The company’s substantial excess capital provides ample opportunities for further investments and capital allocation looking forward.

Other top Q1 contributors included Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Waste Management (WM) and Hca Healthcare (HCA). Health care products manufacturer Abbott Laboratories is executing well, capitalizing on its diverse mix of businesses to drive growth. In Q1, the company announced European approval (CE Mark) for its Volt Pulse Field Ablation (PFA) system, which is designed to provide a new therapy option for people battling heart rhythm disorders such as atrial fibrillation.

Waste collection provider Waste Management benefited in Q1 from its relative insulation from potential tariff-related headwinds. Shares of health care facilities operator HCA Healthcare rebounded in Q1 from a Q4 selloff tied to concerns about heightened policy risks under the new administration, as underlying hospital fundamentals remain strong.

Among our bottom Q1 contributors were lululemon athletica (LULU) and Ferguson Enterprises (FERG). Shares of athletic apparel manufacturer lululemon (a new holding in Q1) were pressured amid ongoing concerns about potential brand saturation in the Americas, its largest market. However, we believe the company is operating well and is positioned to improve US sales performance while continuing to grow international sales — factors which make the current valuation compelling, in our view.

Plumbing and heating products distributor Ferguson Enterprises (another new holding) faced a combination of challenges in the quarter, including deflating commodities- based product prices, which are weighing on revenues, combined with management’s ongoing future-oriented investments, which are pressuring margins. In a fragmented industry, we believe there remains ample opportunity for Ferguson to benefit from a virtuous cycle of margin expansion leading to an improved share price in the period ahead.

Other bottom Q1 contributors included General Motors (GM), Martin Marietta Materials (MLM) and Texas Instruments (TXN).

Automobile manufacturer General Motors faces heightened uncertainty surrounding new tariffs along with overall macroeconomic concerns, which weighed on shares during the quarter. Martin Marietta Materials — a new position in the quarter — is a heavy building materials supplier and the second-largest aggregates producer and distributor in the US. Shares were pressured amid broader macroeconomic uncertainty — however, we believe in the company’s high quality and strong brand in an industry with a deep moat. Semiconductor manufacturer Texas Instruments faced what we believe are some near-term headwinds, which weighed on shares in the quarter; however, we maintain our long-term outlook on the company’s growth prospects.

Portfolio Activity

As volatility picked up sharply in the quarter, we were active in the portfolio — and we anticipate that as volatility continues into Q2, we will likewise attempt to capitalize on compelling opportunities to reposition the portfolio for the period ahead. As mentioned earlier, we initiated new positions in Martin Marietta Materials, lululemon athletica and Ferguson Enterprises, among others, in Q1.

Martin Marietta Materials is a heavy building materials supplier and the second-largest aggregates producer and distributor in the US with competitively positioned cement, ready-mix, asphalt and magnesia specialty businesses. The company has a long-term mindset, which proved effective during the last recession. Generally, the company tries to be the price leader in its markets and has historically successfully passed inflationary prices along to its clients.

Given its strategic positioning in markets with strong growth potential and in states with approved multi-year Department of Transportation plans, we believe it is well-positioned for the period ahead. Recent macroeconomic uncertainty has pressured shares, introducing a compelling opportunity for us to invest in a high-quality aggregates company at a good entry point.

lululemon athletica is a leading technical apparel brand with strong affinity in the Americas and a growing presence internationally. As mentioned earlier, shares were pressured in the quarter as investors weighed the possibility that the brand’s health is faltering amid heightened competition.

However, we believe these challenges are largely transitory and margins are sustainable, giving us an attractive entry point in what we believe is a high-quality, cash-generative company with a clean balance sheet.

Ferguson is a leading US distributor of plumbing, waterworks, HVAC and related products. The company bridges the gap between large and fragmented sets of customers and suppliers and is the market leader in an industry in which scale matters. While the company has benefited from strong macroeconomic tailwinds recently, which may soften over the next year or two, we believe this possibility is largely reflected in the share price, which was a key reason we were able to initiate a position at an opportunistic price.

We funded these purchases in part with the proceeds from our sale of internet retail and cloud infrastructure company Amazon (AMZN), whose shares were approaching our estimate of intrinsic value. We also exited our positions in heavy construction machinery manufacturer Caterpillar (CAT), copper- focused mining company Freeport-McMoRan (FCX), self-storage real estate investment trust Extra Space Storage (EXR) and home improvement retailer Home Depot (HD) in order to reallocate our capital to more attractive investment opportunities.

Market Outlook

As we start Q2, uncertainty is as high as it has been, arguably since the pandemic’s earliest days. While markets struggle to digest the likeliest ramifications of the US’s newly announced tariffs — whether they are largely a negotiation tactic whose economic impact will moderate over time as countries and companies reach better terms with the US, or whether they are here to stay for the long term and we will indeed see a significant reshaping of the world trade order — we are watching several factors particularly closely. Foremost, we are monitoring the relationship between the US and China, which is front and center in this trade showdown. As we write, President Trump has threatened a 104% tariff on China in response to its retaliatory increase to 84%. Given the magnitude of the US and Chinese economies, seeing the two countries enter a full-blown trade war would have significant, widespread implications for many US companies, up and down the capitalization spectrum.

Further, the increased uncertainty across a range of macroeconomic policies — trade as well as domestic spending and the administration’s ongoing efforts to meaningfully shrink the size of the federal government — is prompting concerns about the consumer. Should tariffs prompt a significant spike in inflation and/or fiscal spending cuts go too far, consumers could rein in their spending.

Indeed, companies across various sectors have noted a weakening consumer, suggesting the economy is slowing. With the consumer powering a significant majority of the economy, further weakening may present a risk to the lofty double-digit corporate earnings growth the market had been expecting for 2025.

However, regardless of how uncomfortable such volatility can be, we are simultaneously encouraged by its impact on the valuations of some companies that we believe are particularly strong and well-positioned to weather the environment ahead — even if it requires further suffering before economic and market growth resume. Accordingly, we have sought to be active in this period, identifying high- quality companies with particularly solid balance sheets whose outlook over the next three to five years is positive in our view. As downward volatility has continued, we have further acted on our research efforts to introduce these names to the portfolio, with the aim of positioning the portfolio well for whatever the environment gives us in the period ahead — whether there is further downside to endure, or whether markets turn around precipitously. We believe, with hindsight, that this period may prove to be one that separates those investors with a rigorous, bottom-up process from those unwilling or unable to see through the near-term volatility to the possibilities beyond.

Our primary focus is always on achieving value-added results for our existing clients, and we believe we can achieve better-than-market returns over the next five years through active portfolio management.
